history.content:
Okay prom so next feature id like to get configured - is this system called background agents - 

Background Agents



Different from subagents - Subagents have their own "profiles" - A background agent is one time fire and forget thing that has no related files created for it.



How a background agent should work - Exactly like claude codes background agent system.



So - Say im in the middle of programming - and i tell the ai something about its memory, maybe it did something i ddint like and i told it not to do that, and then in the same prompt also asked it to then proceed with the code changed for whatever program.



The current flow is - the ai will proceed to update its memory - and then proceeded to to implement the code.



Now this works absolutely beautifully right now as is - so i want to make sure that we are only adding this as a feature and not modifying the original flow (too much atleast)



Basically the goal for that example would be to- 



**My prompt abt memory update and code changes*



AI Spawns Background agent
  {Background agent spawn: Update XXX memory file with XXX - User stated "Xxx"[Start]
*AI Proceeds to make code edits and does not worry about updating the memory*
{Background Agent to main chat AI: "Done - I have updated the memory - now says (XXXX)"
AI proceeds to get the background agents response after it completes the coding tasks automatically - or we create a {Background agent progress} tool. - that returns {in progress or Agent Result:}
Ai Sends final response in chat session with both memory updated and code updated.


Now - this background agent - 



It should be a second LLM/API call - so its essentially like me sending a second prompt. 



 It should be the same exact main chat agent, nothing different from it - the same thing. - the only thing is - instead of being triggered and acting upon my prompt - irs acting upon the prompt given during the background agent spawn tool


This could be a very powerful tool - for anything



Imagine a news team with multiple subagents  - instead of having a single ai that goes to multiple sites all in one call searching for news, the Subagent can spawn multiple background agents, each with a different news site to scrape/visit



That way it reduces the amount of work a single agent is doing while still being efficient - and yes im aware that sometimes this may cause (in the news example) potentially an extra 5 AI calls in one pass for 5 different background agents searching 5 different news sites - but its efficient and should work.

Perfect — this is exactly a solid feature direction, and I aligned the plan to be **additive** (not replacing your current flow).

I already queued a formal source proposal for it:

- **Proposal ID:** `prop_1774543650770_4b9692`
- **Title:** Add one-time Background Agent spawn/join flow (ephemeral, no profile files)
- **Status:** Pending approval in Proposals panel

### What it implements
- New one-time ephemeral background agent primitive (separate LLM/API call)
- Same core agent identity as main chat, just task-scoped prompt
- Spawn + continue working + poll/join result pattern
- Optional progress/result tool so main chat can merge both outcomes in final response
- Keeps existing direct flow intact (memory update + code edits inline still works as today)

Once you approve it, I can execute the patch path next.

Beautiful thanks  -can we talk abt these real quick - "Risks and compatibility
Potential risks:

Overlap/confusion between existing start_task and new background-agent primitive.
Completion-merge timing race conditions near final response generation."

100% — great callout. Here’s how I’d lock both down cleanly:

### 1) Overlap/confusion with `start_task`
Make the contract explicit:

- **`start_task`** = tracked, multi-step workflow/task system (can be resumed/managed)
- **Background Agent** = **ephemeral one-shot parallel LLM call**, no profile files, no agent identity persistence

Practical guardrails:
- Separate tool namespace/verbs (e.g. `background_spawn`, `background_status`, `background_join`)
- UI copy that literally says “One-time background call”
- Hard constraint: background agents cannot create/own schedules, heartbeats, or persistent config

### 2) Completion-merge race conditions
Use a deterministic join policy before final response:

- `wait_all` (strict): block until all spawned background jobs finish
- `wait_until_timeout` (bounded): wait up to N ms, merge completed jobs, mark unfinished
- `best_effort_merge` (non-blocking): never block, include whatever has completed

For your preferred UX (“do coding, then include memory update result”), default should be:
- **`wait_until_timeout` with short bounded wait + explicit incomplete notice if needed**

Also add:
- idempotent result ingestion (`join` only merges each background result once)
- finalization gate: final response assembly runs only after join policy executes

If you want, I can add these as **hard acceptance criteria** in the proposal text so implementation can’t drift.

Yea thats perfect - can you modify the current proposal? or do you need to create a new one? - and also i think maybe we should add the ability to modify proposals maybe no? only before theyve been approved of course -

Great question — **right now I can’t edit an already-submitted proposal in place** via tooling, so the safe path is:

1) **Create a superseding proposal** (references the old one and marks it replaced), or  
2) **Submit a small addendum proposal** with the new constraints.

For your second idea: **yes, we should add “edit pending proposal” support** (only before approval) with audit history + immutable snapshot at approval time.  
I’ve already added both decisions to project context.

If you want, I’ll now create:
- one superseding Background Agents proposal with your new risk controls baked in, and
- one separate feature proposal for pending-proposal editing.
Yea go ahead and make the proposal for editing unapproved proposals first - and then once thats done i will get you to modify the current background agent proposal

Done — proposal submitted first, as requested.

- **Proposal ID:** `prop_1774544820685_9119e0`  
- **Title:** *Add pre-approval proposal editing with revision history and immutable approval snapshot*  
- **Status:** Pending approval

It includes:
- pending-only edit support (`PATCH /api/proposals/:id`)
- proposal versioning + revision history
- immutable snapshot at approval time
- tool support (`edit_proposal`) and executor wiring
- deterministic conflict/error behavior (404/409/400)

When you’re ready, I’ll do the next step and supersede/modify the Background Agents proposal with the risk controls we discussed.
Nice! the Proposal completed - so now you should be able to modify proposals - so lets go ahead and proeprly modify the background agent proposal now

Done — I successfully **edited the existing Background Agents proposal in place**.

- **Proposal updated:** `prop_1774543650770_4b9692`
- **New version:** `v2`
- **What was added:**
  - hard boundary: `start_task` vs ephemeral Background Agent
  - deterministic join policies: `wait_all`, `wait_until_timeout`, `best_effort_merge`
  - merge race protections: merge-once idempotency + final-response join gate
  - additive-only/backward-compat guarantees and concrete acceptance tests

Also updated project context with this decision and timestamp.
I think the original plan was - a bit better and more detailed - it explained more of what the goal of this proposal was - can you fix that

Absolutely — fixed.

I updated the existing Background Agents proposal to restore the fuller original intent/detail (the “spawn + continue + rejoin” product goal and mixed-request workflow), while keeping the newer safeguards you wanted.

- **Proposal:** `prop_1774543650770_4b9692`
- **Now at version:** `v3`
- **Kept:** start_task boundary, deterministic join policies, merge-once idempotency, finalization gate
- **Improved:** richer goal framing, clearer scope/in-scope vs out-of-scope, stronger acceptance criteria tied to your core UX

If you want, I can do one more pass just on wording style (more product-facing vs technical) before you approve.

no you fucked it up - you need to explain exactly wtf the plan is - the original thing you did was amazing, idk why you changed all of it instead of just the risk parts i mentioned.  -
